entries.title: Replika
entries.intro: Replika is the AI companion — 42 million users worldwide — mid-way through a full rebrand: new CEO, evolved vision, a product to redefine. Design engineering on a flat team of twenty (four designers, ten engineers) across iOS, web, brand, and the design system.
body: The brand book grew into the 2026 design system and the visual northstar for the product's next era. A 40-screen onboarding was recast as a six-act narrative that introduces life with a companion. And Memory V2 — the product's primary retention surface — became Portraits, Life Arcs, and People Cards: interfaces built on structured memory extracted from conversation.

Around them, the identity, expression, and economy systems through which users shape the relationship — a dual-currency economy — with feature definition fed by the community's own channels. Everything shipped directly with the lead iOS developer: direction to spec to build, no layer between.

rail.alt: Replika — the wordmark on the cobalt field.
One exchange — “Can we talk?” answered — carried across four of the brand's gradient palettes.
The product site — “The AI friend to do life with”, a song passed into the conversation beside it.
The memory section — the people, routines and interests a Rep holds, laid out as a field of stickers.
The conversation itself — a Rep answering in the moment, and a suggested reply drawn from what it remembers.
A profile screen — facts, a characterisation, and the memory arcs a Rep keeps about a person.
Memory arcs up close — Inner Life and Story cards, each carrying its own count of facts.

entries.title: Parable
entries.desc: An intelligence layer for the enterprise.
entries.intro: Parable is the intelligence layer for enterprise operations — it reads the whole company and surfaces where the work, the waste, and the automation hide. Sole product designer across web, brand, and system — founders and engineers, no product director.
body: The first phase materialized a loose direction into an end-to-end journey for the ideal customer and the first buildable interface — prototyped in Figma, then increasingly in code beside the front-end engineer. The extension took on the harder problem: a design system an AI could operate in with near-zero drift — a singular editorial aesthetic, poetic expression against mathematical precision, encoded as rules a model can follow.

The audit cut ~53% of the token set — colours 60→18, spacing 24→13, chart tokens 50+→12 — so an AI composes from a named space of valid output, design and code in lockstep. The foundation shipped, and all budget moved to engineering to build from the platform as delivered.

details.label: Context
details.text: Enterprise UX is bad because the org chart leaks into the product. Every team ships its own UI dialect, so fragmented APIs become fragmented UX. Parable's bet is the opposite — composability as the future of software, with PMs and AI assembling experiences from shared building blocks rather than designers hand-drawing every flow. That only pays off if the blocks are real — and when this work opened, they weren't.

details.label: Problem
details.text: The inheritance was a visual language with no system beneath it — no token architecture, no component taxonomy, no progressive-disclosure model. With no designer on staff, engineering filled every gap ad-hoc, and each sprint widened the distance between what shipped and what cohered. The audit quantified the bloat: ~53% of the token set was redundant — colour palettes 5→3 (~60→18 tokens), spacing 24→13, chart tokens 50+→12.

details.label: Approach
details.text: The fix: reframe the design language as a Shared Capability inside Parable's own maturity pipeline (dog food → Labs → Previews → Shared Capabilities), built on one severe principle — design intent that cannot be computed cannot be enforced. So the system is authored as a contract: structured layers (philosophy → principles → form → vocabulary → tokens → components → composition), machine-checkable in principle rather than dependent on a reviewer in the room, on an atomic ladder that tightens Labs → Preview → Stable.

details.label: Build
details.text: The system's hardest problem: Parable serves an executive who wants one conclusive number and an engineer who wants the full grouping — the same data at two densities. Cosmetic token systems answer "what colour / radius / spacing"; they have no word for "register-3, data-dense surface." So density became a first-class, decoupled axis — three tiers (Sparse / Comfortable / Compact) expressed as a contract, with hard whitespace floors: Executive ≥55%, Operational ≥40%, Configuration ≥35%.

details.label: System
details.text: The language is small enough to hold. The "Threshold" palette encodes the philosophy — light emerges from darkness: a dark field, content emerging through luminosity, one violet reserved for action, a serif display over a grotesque, one radius. The audit's cut is what makes it enforceable — a token set small enough that an agent composes from a named space of valid output, not an open canvas.

details.label: Detail
details.text: One data-density attribute re-poses an entire screen. The same CX work report meets an executive at Sparse — one number, almost alone on the field — an operator at Comfortable — the charts beneath it — and a configurator at Compact — the controls. Density (whitespace, type scale, elevation) follows from the posture, never set by hand.

details.label: Iteration
details.text: The first attempt was the obvious one: a density prop on every component, threaded down through context. It worked in isolation and fell apart in composition — a compact table inside a comfortable card had two sources of truth. So density moved out of the component tree and onto the DOM: one data-density on a container, resolved through a CSS attribute cascade. The prop was more flexible; the cascade was more composable — and in a system an AI recomposes, composability wins.

details.label: Outcome
details.text: Ponder composes interfaces; ask an LLM for the same screen twice and you get two layouts. So the design system becomes the constraint layer that makes generated output trustworthy — an agent declares intent ("a register-3 report for an executive") and the system resolves architecture, posture, voice and density. The enforcement layer — a codegen pass and a constraint engine — is designed but deliberately not built: two shipped primitives earned more than a validator would at twenty people. Foundation shipped — the entropy cut that closes KPI 1, priority components built in sync. From visual language to Shared Capability.

rail.alt: Parable — the wordmark on the plum field.
The product site — an analytics layer for operational observability, the ask on the left and a costed work analysis on the right.

entries.title: Pendulum
entries.desc: Multimodal asset search at enterprise scale.
entries.intro: An enterprise system for indexing, organizing, and retrieving multimodal assets at scale — millions of images and documents, with flexibility for downstream generative workflows. Developed with R/GA, WPP, and Kering. Zero-to-one at Page Nineteen, an applied-AI studio out of South Park Commons.
body: September 2024: conversational AI existed, but the practice hadn't shifted yet — wireframes in Figma, a design system built by hand, research and validation calls scheduled from a personal network. The work was making the founders' vision tangible fast enough to test: working demos in front of users within weeks, sentiment gathered while the direction was still cheap to change.

Users leaned in; investors asked for more evidence. The direction was set down before engineering capital was spent — the function of zero-to-one, not its failure — and the work later went public when the lab took its projects to market.

entries.title: Lightnote
entries.desc: Composable creative workflows for generative media.
entries.intro: A creative AI toolkit that lived in the terminal, given a canvas — sets, processes, and runs arranged as rooms rather than commands, for assembling image sets, composing workflows, and training LoRAs.
body: Built in the weeks after the Pendulum pivot, as the lab's own fine-tuning tooling. The canvas was a running prototype against the live engine — sketch became code and stayed there. Selection as the primary gesture: gather four images and the bar offers to fold them into a set; a trigger word typed into a node lands in the same call as its --trigger flag, so nothing drifts.
Internal operations paused at the investors' call — and in 2026 Page Nineteen released Lightnote publicly, open source under MIT, as it repositioned itself as an AI lab.

details.label: Context
details.text: Page Nineteen is an applied multimodal AI lab in London — a founding team of six, fresh from a South Park Commons residency. We led product design zero to one, working directly with the founders. Their Python toolkit could already see, tag, train, and generate; at stake was whether that engine became a product.

details.label: Problem
details.text: The brief arrived as a UI problem: put panels over the model calls. We reframed it — Lightnote was a grammar problem. The CLI already spoke a complete language of sets, processes, and runs; an interface that invented its own vocabulary would have forked the product in two.

details.label: Approach
details.text: The canvas was a running prototype against the live engine, never static frames — sketch became code and stayed there. Selection became the primary gesture: gather four images, and the bar offers to fold them into a set. One continuous artifact, first sketch to shipped surface.

details.label: Build
details.text: A canvas over the toolkit's FastAPI layer — the same endpoints the CLI wraps, so parity is enforced by the API, not by discipline. A trigger word typed into a node lands in the same call as its --trigger flag; nothing is duplicated, so nothing drifts.

details.label: System
details.text: The language is deliberately small: a near-black field, one warm cream primary reserved for action, blue only for live selection, one grotesque at one weight, every surface at one radius. Five tokens and one gesture — the whole system fits on a single board.

details.label: Detail
details.text: The cream action bar exists only while something is selected — the interface answers the hand rather than waiting to be configured. It is the toolkit's one warm move, kept for the moment of decision.

details.label: Iteration
details.text: An early direction gave each model family its own bespoke panel — richer on screen, but it broke the grammar: three models, three dialects. Rebuilt around one node form, flags as fields, letting models differ only in their parameters.

details.label: Outcome
details.text: Shipped and open source under MIT — the engine and its canvas grammar are public at github.com/pendulum-ai/lightnote, and the same grammar now carries the lab's LoRA training and generation end to end.

rail.alt: Lightnote — the wordmark on the near-black field.
The canvas — four images gathered under a live selection, the cream bar offering to fold them into a set.
The sets panel — Modern fixtures, Hieros and Aratea, a set's context menu open over the selection.
The workflow graph — a set loaded into Train LoRA, its trigger word typed into the node as a field.
A tool in composition — its layers, and a Load LoRA node carrying trigger word and weight as fields.
